headline: Ex-Administrator and 13 Parents Jailed in ESF Kindergarten Bribery Case
dek: A former administrator at an English Schools Foundation kindergarten has been sentenced to over two years in prison, closing a corruption case that has shaken confidence in Hong Kong's international school admissions system.
author: marcus-chen
heroAlt: Ex-Administrator and 13 Parents Jailed in ESF Kindergarten Bribery Case

body.text: Fatima Rumjahn, a former administrator at the English Schools Foundation's Wu Kai Sha International Kindergarten in Ma On Shan, was jailed for two years and one month on 20 April after pleading guilty to accepting more than HK$640,000 in bribes to move children up the school's admissions waitlist, the Hong Kong Free Press reported. The sentencing came three weeks after thirteen parents and a middleman received terms of up to fourteen months for their role in the same scheme.

The case, which stemmed from an ICAC investigation triggered by a corruption complaint, centres on events between September 2018 and August 2021, when Rumjahn handled admissions at the kindergarten. Individual bribes ranged from HK$20,000 to HK$200,000 and were paid to secure places for twelve children who had cleared the K1 process for the 2019 to 2022 school years but landed at the bottom of the waiting list.

Rumjahn pleaded guilty in October 2024 and testified against the fourteen defendants, four fathers and nine mothers, aged between 36 and 48, as well as a middleman, Siu Yu-pong, who received the longest sentence of fourteen months after helping a business partner's daughter secure admission. Deputy District Judge Amy Chan, in handing down Rumjahn's sentence, noted that she had "took advantage of the parents' wish to provide better education for their children and proactively offered to help them jump the queue."

The first arrests in the case were made in June 2022. That timeline, nearly four years from arrest to the final sentence, reflects the complexity of prosecuting admissions corruption at an institution that is not formally selective but operates, in practice, through a heavily oversubscribed waitlist system. ESF schools are non-selective in principle, but popular entry points such as K1, Year 1 and Year 7 routinely attract far more applicants than available places.

The sentences arrive at a delicate moment for ESF. The group is simultaneously managing record-high financial reserves, a proposed fee increase for 2026–27, and its own acknowledgement in this year's annual report that it faces stiffer competition for students from a growing field of independent international schools. How it responds, procedurally and communicatively, to the reputational shadow cast by the bribery case will be closely watched by the families it is now actively courting.

For the broader international school community in Hong Kong, the case is a reminder that the scarcity of premium English-medium places creates conditions in which admissions integrity requires active management, not merely assumed good faith. Several independent operators have in recent years tightened their admissions governance in response to regulatory signals from the Education Bureau, and the ESF case is likely to accelerate those conversations.
